en.extract:
The Zapata wren (Ferminia cerverai) is a medium-sized grayish-brown bird that lives in dense shrubs of the Zapata Swamp, Cuba. It is the only member of the monotypical genus Ferminia, which is endemic to Cuba and endangered. This species was first described in 1926 by Thomas Barbour and named after its co-discoverer, Fermín Zanón Cervera.


Description
Measuring about 16 centimetres (6.3 in) in length, it is brown overall, though striped with black and with grayish underparts. Its tail is long. The bird's song is similar to that of the house wren, in that it is high-pitched and loud, described as a "musical warble preceded by guttural note, given in series of three or four phrases."


Habitat and conservation
The Zapata wren is confined and endemic to the Zapata Peninsula of southern Cuba. The Zapata wren's habitat is typically freshwater marsh and lowland savanna with scattered bushes and low trees. It feeds on insects, spiders, small snails, lizards and berries. The wren typically makes its nest in sawgrass tussocks. It is thought to breed between January and July.
Typical threats are fires in the dry season, drainage of the wetlands, destruction due to agriculture, and predation by introduced mongooses and rats.


Discovery
The Zapata wren was formally described by American herpetologist Thomas Barbour, who gave it the specific name cerverai in honour of the wren's discoverer, Fermín Zanón Cervera, a Spaniard who had stayed on after the Spanish–American War and become a professional naturalist. Barbour had been accompanied by Cervera on his previous visits to Cuba, and on hearing of the strange birds to be found in the Zapata area, he sent the Spaniard on a series of trips into the region, eventually leading to the finding of the wren. In Spanish, the wren is known simply as ferminia.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet
Image of Cervera and his Zapata birds at Birding Cuba
Zapata wren videos on the Internet Bird Collection

The '''Zapata wren''' ('''''Ferminia cerverai''''') is a medium-sized grayish-brown bird that lives in dense [[shrub]]s of the [[Zapata Swamp]], [[Cuba]]. It is the only member of the [[Monotypic taxon|monotypical]] [[genus]] ''Ferminia'', which is endemic to Cuba and [[endangered]]. This species was first described in 1926 by [[Thomas Barbour]] and named after its co-discoverer, [[Fermín Zanón Cervera]].

==Description==
Measuring about {{convert|16|cm|in}} in length, it is brown overall, though striped with black and with grayish underparts. Its tail is long. The bird's [[Bird vocalization|song]] is similar to that of the [[Northern house wren|house wren]], in that it is high-pitched and loud, described as a "musical warble preceded by guttural note, given in series of three or four phrases."{{cn|date=November 2019}}

[[File:Zapata wren DSCN4200.jpg|thumb|Zapata wren, 2025]]

==Habitat and conservation==
The Zapata wren is confined and [[Endemism in birds|endemic]] to the [[Zapata Peninsula]] of southern Cuba. The Zapata wren's [[habitat]] is typically freshwater [[marsh]] and lowland [[savanna]] with scattered bushes and low trees. It feeds on [[insect]]s, [[spider]]s, small snails, [[lizard]]s and [[berries]]. The wren typically makes its nest in [[Cladium|sawgrass tussocks]]. It is thought to breed between January and July.

Typical threats are fires in the [[dry season]], drainage of the [[wetland]]s, destruction due to agriculture, and [[predation]] by introduced mongooses and rats.

==Discovery==
The Zapata wren was formally described by American [[herpetology|herpetologist]] [[Thomas Barbour]],<ref name= barbour>{{cite journal | last= Barbour | first= Thomas | year= 1926| journal= Proceedings of the New England Zoological Club | volume= 9 |pages = 73–75|title=A remarkable new bird from Cuba}}</ref> who gave it the specific name ''cerverai'' in honour of the wren's discoverer, [[Fermín Zanón Cervera]], a Spaniard who had stayed on after the [[Spanish–American War]] and become a professional [[naturalist]]. Barbour had been accompanied by Cervera on his previous visits to Cuba, and on hearing of the strange birds to be found in the Zapata area, he sent the Spaniard on a series of trips into the region, eventually leading to the finding of the wren.<ref name=peters >{{cite journal | last= Peters | first= James l |date=July 1948| title= Thomas Barbour, 1884–1946 | journal= The Auk | volume= 65| issue = 3 | pages= 432–438| url =http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v065n03/p0432-p0438.pdf | doi= 10.2307/4080492| jstor= 4080492 }}</ref> In Spanish, the wren is known simply as ''ferminia''.

zh.extract:
扎巴鹪鹩（台湾作札帕塔鷦鷯，学名：Ferminia cerverai），是雀形目鹪鹩科的一种鸟类，属于单型的扎巴鹪鹩属（学名：Ferminia）。


特征
扎巴鹪鹩体重约26克，翼长约52毫米，喙宽度约3毫米，喙厚度约4.5毫米，嘴峰长约17.2毫米，跗蹠长约22毫米，尾长约62毫米。
扎巴鹪鹩是留鸟，栖息在湖泊、沼泽等湿地环境，它们是食肉动物，主要以昆虫、蜘蛛等陆生无脊椎动物为食。它们分布在古巴西南部。
